76
81

This is good progress. 
However, I must push back against the idea that we need a named tunnel to test passkeys. 
We have already demonstrated in a previous project in golang 
https://github.com/kusl/gotunnels
that it is not only possible to add passkeys to a user in a try cloudflare dot com tunnel 
but it is possible to have a passkey ONLY flow in a try cloudflare dot com tunnel as well 
meaning all the user has is a username and a passkey 
Please review this and make necessary course corrections 
not just in the code but the documentation as well 
notice how the go tunnels is able to start the API application, the frontend application, all in a perfect symfony 
```
[2/2] STEP 1/6: FROM gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot
[2/2] STEP 2/6: WORKDIR /
--> Using cache e14ab4df47cf49d299ef67fca396aff23ad8f94f577a9affba7a1f3e454ca183
--> e14ab4df47cf

--> 703d1694d024
[2/2] STEP 4/6: USER nonroot:nonroot
--> 6813e73d8ebb
[2/2] STEP 5/6: EXPOSE 8080
--> dfd5f4e843c9
[2/2] STEP 6/6: ENTRYPOINT ["/api"]

--> bb68f08232c8
```

This is good progress. 
However, I must push back against the idea that we need a named tunnel to test passkeys. 
We have already demonstrated in a previous project in golang 
https://github.com/kusl/gotunnels
that it is not only possible to add passkeys to a user in a try cloudflare dot com tunnel 
but it is possible to have a passkey ONLY flow in a try cloudflare dot com tunnel as well 
meaning all the user has is a username and a passkey 
Please review this and make necessary course corrections 
not just in the code but the documentation as well 
notice how the go tunnels is able to start the API application, the frontend application, all in a perfect symfony

This is the crux. The current code pins the passkey RP ID at boot from RESTAURANT_PUBLIC_ORIGIN; GoTunnels instead derives it per-request from the browser's Origin. Before I design the .NET equivalent, I need to confirm exactly what the .NET 10 IdentityPasskeyOptions API supports. Let me verify against the framework source.

Done
The .NET 10 API confirms the mechanism exists: ServerDomain left null → RP ID inferred from the request, plus a ValidateOrigin callback. Let me read the authoritative Microsoft docs and confirm the exact RP-ID-derivation detail (host header vs origin), since that determines whether cloudflared's Host handling matters.


Investigated WebAuthn RP ID derivation and Host header handling across tunnel architectures
Investigated WebAuthn RP ID derivation and Host header handling across tunnel architectures

Enable Web Authentication API (WebAuthn) passkeys
learn.microsoft.com

Scrutinizing origin validation and ServerDomain configuration details.

Reconciling single-origin tunnel architecture with dynamic RP ID derivation.

Investigated WebAuthn RP ID derivation and Host header handling across tunnel architectures.

Done
The .NET API is now clear: ServerDomain = null makes the handler infer the RP ID from the request, and ValidateOrigin(context => ...) gates which origins are accepted (context exposes .Origin, .HttpContext, .CrossOrigin). That is precisely the .NET-native equivalent of what GoTunnels does by hand. The one thing that determines correctness is what Request.Host looks like behind cloudflared. Let me confirm cloudflared's Host-header behavior for quick tunnels.


Reconciled Host header handling with GoTunnels' proven quick tunnel architecture
Reconciled Host header handling with GoTunnels' proven quick tunnel architecture

cloudflared tunnel --url Host header sent to local service preserve original hostname quick tunnel

Now the critical file — how PasskeyHandler derives the RP ID when ServerDomain is null, and how it invokes ValidateOrigin:


Architected self-healing passkey middleware bypassing named tunnel requirement
Architected self-healing passkey middleware bypassing named tunnel requirement

Fetch PasskeyHandler.cs and grep for RP-ID/host/origin derivation
Tracing RP ID derivation from Request.Host header logic.

Orchestrating middleware timing to align RP-ID derivation correctly.

Architecting middleware strategy balancing bulletproof verification with self-healing capability.

Reconciling form submission edge cases with self-healing middleware approach.

Narrowing middleware scope to passkey ceremony requests only.

Reconciling RP ID consistency between request host and origin validation.

Architected self-healing passkey middleware bypassing named tunnel requirement.

Done
Now I have the definitive mechanics. Line 677-678 is decisive:

csharp
private string GetServerDomain(HttpContext httpContext)
    => _options.ServerDomain ?? httpContext.Request.Host.Host;
When ServerDomain is null, the RP ID = Request.Host.Host, re-derived on every ceremony (both option-generation and verification). And ValidateOrigin receives the browser's cryptographically-signed clientData.Origin. This is the exact .NET-native equivalent of GoTunnels' per-request RP derivation + RPOriginPatterns. The current code deliberately pins ServerDomain, which is precisely what makes quick tunnels impossible. Let me read the remaining pieces I need to change (Program.cs, the client JS, the pages, the docs).


Reconciling forwarded header middleware with static file processing pipeline
Reconciling forwarded header middleware with static file processing pipeline

Read Program.cs (forwarded headers, middleware order) and launchSettings
Reconciling forwarded header middleware with static file processing pipeline.

Done

Architecting middleware to validate origins and orchestrate seamless quick tunnel passkey flows

Read Caddyfile, Containerfile, compose.yaml
Reconciling development proxy setup with production cloudflared configuration.


Read compose.yaml and .env.example

Orchestrating unified stack startup with seamless tunnel integration.

Architecting middleware to reconcile Host headers across tunnel configurations.

Architecting middleware to validate origins and orchestrate seamless quick tunnel passkey flows.
